game_name: Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles: Tournament Fighters

danger_signals.visual_form: Shredder/Cyber Shredder as final opponent with distinct visual design
danger_signals.meaning: Represents primary antagonist threat; final challenge tier

danger_signals.visual_form: Monstrous/alien character designs (War, Armaggon, Wingnut, Chrome Dome)
danger_signals.meaning: Visual distinction of hostile entities through grotesque or mechanical forms

danger_signals.visual_form: Same character with different color palette (flickering on NES during Hothead clones)
danger_signals.meaning: Visual signal of corrupted/duplicated threat; technical manifestation of danger intensity

safety_signals.visual_form: Four core TMNT characters (Leonardo, Raphael, Michelangelo, Donatello) as player avatars
safety_signals.meaning: Player agency and heroic identity; safety through control of established heroes

safety_signals.visual_form: Flying monitor with Splinter's face displaying red ball power-up
safety_signals.meaning: Splinter as mentor figure appearing during danger; resource availability signals player can recover strength

safety_signals.visual_form: April O'Neil and Splinter as rescue targets in story mode
safety_signals.meaning: Presence of allies and civilian protection goals establish moral framework and world stakes

faction_indicators.color_identity: Individual color-coding per turtle (Leonardo=blue, Raphael=red, Michelangelo=orange, Donatello=purple)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Clone versions of turtles with alternate color palettes represent corrupted/enemy versions
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Turtle Blimp as travel/faction base in SNES story mode

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Chrome Dome (android created by Shredder); Karai's forces (antagonist faction in story mode)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Tournament structure as Shredder's challenge mechanism

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Diverse character designs (War=purple monstrous form, Aska=ninja girl, Wingnut=bat-alien, Armaggon=mutant shark)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Original game characters created for tournament context (Aska, Hothead)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Tournament venue as neutral ground where multiple factions converge

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Tournament structure; organized bracket format in fighting game UI
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Formal combat rules; one-on-one matches; turn-based confrontation system

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Shredder/Cyber Shredder presence as boss tier opponent; escalating opponent difficulty
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Turtles forced to participate in tournament to confront nemesis; defensive/reactive posture

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: April O'Neil and Splinter captured by Karai; map travel via Turtle Blimp showing geographic scope
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Rescue mission objective; cross-country travel; information gathering during fights

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Four Turtles competing against each other in tournament format
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Intra-faction combat to determine leadership/worthiness; self-testing before external threat

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Screen flicker during Hothead vs. Hothead matches; sprite flicker during multi-character scenes
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Slowdown effects; reduced animation smoothness; environmental response to character size/quantity

environmental_storytelling.example: Presence of Chrome Dome (Shredder-created android) indicates Shredder's technological capabilities and past actions within tournament context
Tournament structure organizes all character encounters; serves as mechanism for Turtles to confront Shredder while testing themselves against diverse fighters
SNES story mode shows Turtles traveling across US in Turtle Blimp, establishing continental scope and connectivity of world

environmental_storytelling.technique: Ally capture as world state indicator
environmental_storytelling.example: April O'Neil and Splinter being held by Karai establishes current threat level and creates story urgency/stakes
Archie Comics-sourced characters (War, Wingnut) vs. animated series characters (Chrome Dome) vs. original game characters (Aska) indicate different narrative source layers

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Appears as power-up monitor in battle (NES); becomes rescue objective in SNES story mode; visual presence as floating face signals guidance and world protection concern
Serves as final boss; organizes/participates in tournament; forces Turtles into competitive framework; Cyber Shredder variant indicates technological enhancement
Captures April and Splinter; drives SNES story mode narrative; represents Shredder faction extension

Can fight each other (NES mode shows intra-faction matches); travel across country (SNES); color-differentiated to enable visual distinction during clones; individual move sets create personality expression

entity_behavioral_tells.entity: Independent Fighters (Aska, Hothead, Wingnut, Armaggon, War, Chrome Dome)
entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Neutral opponents; represent diverse threat types through visual design (monstrous, mechanical, alien); serve as progression gates before faction-specific battles

notes: TMNT: Tournament Fighters (Genesis) uses tournament framework as primary world-state communication device. Visual danger signals manifest through boss hierarchy (Shredder as apex threat), enemy design diversity (monstrous/alien/mechanical forms), and character cloning (corruption/doubling). Faction identity relies on color-coding (Turtles) and mechanical/monstrous design (Shredder's forces). World state shifts between competitive (tournament), crisis (story mode capture), and internal conflict (intra-Turtle matches) are visually signaled through opponent roster, environmental objectives (rescue), and geographic scope (Turtle Blimp travel). The game treats visual design as primary communication method given fighting game genre constraints on narrative exposition.
